title: Sunday, November 26
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: November 26, 2017 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Last Sunday after Pentecost, also known as Christ the King Sunday, the final Sunday of Ordinary Time before Advent begins. The RCL readings for this date were Ezekiel 34:11-16, 20-24 (God as shepherd), Psalm 100, Ephesians 1:15-23 (spiritual blessings in Christ), and Matthew 25:31-46 (the judgment of nations/parable of the sheep and goats). This is one of the most theologically weighty Sundays of the year, focusing on Christ's kingship and final judgment, making it a natural occasion for preaching about faith, justice, and Christian responsibility in the world.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of the 79th General Convention held in Austin, Texas in June-July 2017, which had approved significant resolutions on immigration, criminal justice reform, and climate change. The Diocese of Colorado (within which St. John's Cathedral operates) was navigating questions about church engagement with national political divisions, particularly around immigration policy and racial justice. There were no major diocesan bishop elections or crises immediately surrounding this date, but the broader church was wrestling with how to respond to the Trump administration's policies on these issues.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical late November weather patterns, with winter approaching and holiday season preparations underway. The city was focused on holiday shopping and Thanksgiving aftermath, with minimal major civic crises in the immediate pre-sermon week. The Capitol Hill neighborhood where the Cathedral is located was stable, though the city continued ongoing debates about homelessness and housing affordability that had characterized 2017.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was focused on the unfolding sexual harassment and assault scandals in Hollywood and politics following the Harvey Weinstein revelations from October; the Alabama Senate race between Roy Moore (Republican, facing allegations of sexual misconduct) and Doug Jones (Democrat) was in its final weeks before the December 12 election. The Trump administration was preparing for major tax reform legislation that would be passed in Congress within weeks. The Russia investigation led by Special Counsel Robert Mueller was active in the news cycle. The national mood was fractured and polarized heading into the final weeks before Christmas.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Myanmar's military had recently expelled the Rohingya Muslim population in a campaign described by the UN as genocide; over 700,000 refugees had fled to Bangladesh in preceding months. North Korea continued its missile tests and weapons development under Kim Jong-un, with heightened tensions between Washington and Pyongyang. In Iraq and Syria, the campaign against ISIS was in its final phases, with territorial gains being made by coalition forces.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American society in late November 2017 was characterized by deep political division, reckoning with sexual violence and power imbalances (the #MeToo movement was gaining momentum), and uncertainty about the direction of the country under the Trump presidency. Christmas commercialization was beginning in earnest. The mood was anxious yet resilient, with many Americans seeking spiritual grounding and community. A sermon on Christ the King and final judgment would have resonated against a backdrop of Americans questioning justice, accountability, and moral authority in their institutions.
